schools.summary: The sin of concealing testimony under adjuration: 'and if a soul sin, and hear the voice of swearing, and is a witness, whether he hath seen or known of it; if he do not utter it, then he shall bear his iniquity' — if a man, hearing the voice of adjuration (a public charge or oath put to witnesses to testify), being a witness who had seen or known the matter, did not utter (testify) it, he sinned and bore his iniquity (the adjuration, the public oath calling on witnesses to speak, Matthew 26:63, the concealing, the witness staying silent, suppressing the truth, the guilt, the sin of silence, the duty to testify neglected); the witness under adjuration (bound to testify, the truth required) — the duty (the obligation to bear witness to the truth, not to conceal it, the lesson, the sin of suppressing truth, the duty of honest testimony, the guilt of silence when called to speak, Proverbs 29:24); 'then he shall bear his iniquity' (the bearing of the guilt, the sin of concealment, the iniquity borne until atoned, the figure, the sins of omission, the neglect of duty, needing atonement, the providence, the trespass offering for the sin of silence, vv.5-6) — Henry, Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

themes: And if a soul sin, and hear the voice of swearing, and is a witness, whether he hath seen or known of it; if he do not utter it, then he shall bear his iniquity
the sin of concealing testimony under adjuration
the witness bound to testify, the guilt of suppressing the truth (Matthew 26:63)
a sin of omission and neglected duty needing atonement (Proverbs 29:24)

schools.summary: The sin of touching the unclean unawares: 'or if a soul touch any unclean thing, whether it be a carcase of an unclean beast, or a carcase of unclean cattle, or the carcase of unclean creeping things, and if it be hidden from him; he also shall be unclean, and guilty' — if a man touched an unclean thing (the carcase of an unclean beast, cattle, or creeping thing), even unawares (hidden from him), he became unclean and guilty (the touching, the contact with the unclean, defiling, the hidden, done unawares, in ignorance, the guilt, the uncleanness contracted, the guilt real, needing cleansing and atonement); the touching of the unclean (the defilement, the ceremonial uncleanness) — the figure (the defilement of sin, the contact with what is unclean, the pollution of the world, 2 Corinthians 6:17, the need of cleansing); 'if it be hidden from him; he also shall be unclean, and guilty' (the uncleanness even unawares, the guilt real, though hidden, the figure, the defilement of unknown sin, the conscience defiled, the need of cleansing in Christ, 1 John 1:7, the providence, the trespass offering for the uncleanness, the cleansing provided) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: Or if a soul touch any unclean thing... and if it be hidden from him; he also shall be unclean, and guilty
the sin of touching the unclean unawares
the defilement of the unclean even unawares, the conscience defiled
the need of cleansing in Christ (2 Corinthians 6:17; 1 John 1:7)

schools.summary: The sin of touching human uncleanness: 'or if he touch the uncleanness of man, whatsoever uncleanness it be that a man shall be defiled withal, and it be hid from him; when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty' — if a man touched any human uncleanness (the various defilements of man, Leviticus 12-15), even unawares, when he came to know it he was guilty (the human uncleanness, the defilements of the body, disease, issues, death, the hidden, done unawares, the knowing, when the sin came to his knowledge, the guilt acknowledged, vv.5); the touching of human uncleanness (the defilements of the body, Leviticus 12-15) — the figure (the defilement of sin, the pollution contracted); 'when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty' (the guilt on knowing, the conscience awakened, the sin acknowledged, vv.5, the figure, the conviction of sin, the guilt felt when known, the providence, the trespass offering when the sin is known, vv.5-6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Or if he touch the uncleanness of man... when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty
the sin of touching human uncleanness
the defilement of human uncleanness, even unawares (Leviticus 12-15)
the guilt felt when the sin is known, the conviction needing atonement

schools.summary: The sin of the rash oath: 'or if a soul swear, pronouncing with his lips to do evil, or to do good, whatsoever it be that a man shall pronounce with an oath, and it be hid from him; when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty in one of these' — if a man swore rashly (a hasty, thoughtless oath, to do evil or good), and forgot it or failed to keep it, when he knew it he was guilty (the rash oath, the hasty, inconsiderate vow, sworn without thought, the evil or good, any rash promise, whether to harm or to benefit, the guilt, the broken or unwise oath, the sin of the tongue, needing atonement); the rash oath (the hasty vow, sworn without thought, perhaps unkept or unwise) — the lesson (the sin of the unbridled tongue, the rash swearing, the danger of hasty vows, Ecclesiastes 5:2-6, 'be not rash with thy mouth'; Matthew 5:34-37, 'swear not at all'; James 5:12, the warning, against rash oaths and the sins of the tongue); 'when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty in one of these' (the guilt on knowing, the rash oath acknowledged, the sin of the tongue, the figure, the conviction of the sins of the tongue, James 3:2-8, the providence, the trespass offering for the rash oath, vv.5-6) — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: Or if a soul swear, pronouncing with his lips to do evil, or to do good... when he knoweth of it, then he shall be guilty in one of these
the sin of the rash oath
the hasty, thoughtless vow, the sin of the unbridled tongue (Ecclesiastes 5:2-6; James 5:12)
the warning against rash swearing, the sins of the tongue needing atonement (Matthew 5:34-37; James 3:2-8)

schools.summary: The confession of sin: 'and it shall be, when he shall be guilty in one of these things, that he shall confess that he hath sinned in that thing' — the requirement — 'he shall confess that he hath sinned in that thing': before bringing the offering, the guilty man to confess his particular sin (the witnesses noting the significance — the confession of sin, the acknowledgment of the specific sin, named and owned, before the atonement, the lesson — the necessity of confession with the sacrifice, no atonement without the owning of sin, the offering not a mechanical rite but joined with the heart's confession, the confession of the particular sin, 'I have sinned in this thing,' the figure — the confession of sin the way to forgiveness, Proverbs 28:13, 'he that covereth his sins shall not prosper: but whoso confesseth and forsaketh them shall have mercy'; Psalm 32:5, 'I acknowledged my sin... and thou forgavest the iniquity of my sin'; 1 John 1:9, 'if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins,' the heart-confession joined with the blood, the figure of repentance and faith in Christ); the confession of the particular sin (the specific sin named, not a general confession only) — the particularity (the very sin owned, the honest acknowledgment, the lesson, the confession joined with the offering, the heart with the rite); the confession before the offering — the owning of sin (the providence, the confession joined with the sacrifice, the figure of repentance and confession unto forgiveness, 1 John 1:9) — Calvin, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Why must confession accompany the offering?

sides.position: The necessity of confession with the sacrifice - the particular sin owned and named, the offering joined with the heart's acknowledgment, not a mechanical rite; the way to forgiveness (Proverbs 28:13; Psalm 32:5; 1 John 1:9), the figure of repentance and confession joined with faith in the blood of Christ

themes: And it shall be, when he shall be guilty in one of these things, that he shall confess that he hath sinned in that thing
the confession of sin (the crux of confession with the offering)
the particular sin owned and named, the offering joined with the heart's confession
the way to forgiveness, the figure of repentance and faith in Christ (Proverbs 28:13; Psalm 32:5; 1 John 1:9)

schools.summary: The trespass offering and the atonement for sin: 'and he shall bring his trespass offering unto the LORD for his sin which he hath sinned, a female from the flock, a lamb or a kid of the goats, for a sin offering; and the priest shall make an atonement for him concerning his sin' — after confession, the guilty man bringing a female lamb or kid as a trespass (sin) offering, and the priest making atonement (the trespass offering, here a sin offering, for the sins named, vv.1-4, the female lamb or kid, the appointed victim, the atonement, the priest making atonement, the covering of sin, the forgiveness, implied, vv.10, 'it shall be forgiven him'); the trespass offering, here called a sin offering (the offering for the specific trespasses, vv.1-4) — the figure (the one sacrifice for sin, Christ, the offering after confession); 'the priest shall make an atonement for him concerning his sin' (the atonement made, the covering of sin by the blood, the figure, the atonement of Christ, Hebrews 9:22, the providence, the atonement and forgiveness, vv.10, the offering after confession) — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And he shall bring his trespass offering unto the LORD for his sin which he hath sinned, a female from the flock, a lamb or a kid of the goats, for a sin offering; and the priest shall make an atonement for him concerning his sin
the trespass offering and the atonement for sin
the offering brought after confession, for the sins named (vv.1-4)
the atonement made by the blood, the figure of the one sacrifice of Christ (Hebrews 9:22)

schools.summary: The provision for the poor in the trespass offering: 'and if he be not able to bring a lamb, then he shall bring for his trespass, which he hath committed, two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, unto the LORD; one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering' — the mercy — 'if he be not able to bring a lamb, then he shall bring... two turtledoves, or two young pigeons': if the offerer was too poor to bring a lamb, he might bring two birds (one for a sin offering, one for a burnt offering, the witnesses noting the significance — the gracious provision for the poor, the way of atonement adapted to the offerer's ability, the poor not excluded for want of means, the lesson — God's mercy to the poor, the atonement made accessible to all, the lamb or, if too poor, the birds, or, if poorer still, fine flour, vv.11, none shut out from forgiveness by poverty, the gospel preached to the poor, 'the poor have the gospel preached to them,' Matthew 11:5; Luke 4:18, the figure — the two birds, Mary's offering at the purification, being poor, Luke 2:24, 'a pair of turtledoves, or two young pigeons,' the Lord himself born into a poor family that brought the poor's offering, the accessibility of atonement to all, rich and poor); the two birds (one for sin, one for the burnt offering, the atonement and the consecration) — the figure (the sin borne and the whole devotion, the two aspects of the one atonement, the providence, the poor's offering, the two birds, the atonement for the poor); the birds for the poor — the gracious adaptation (the providence, the way of atonement for the poor, God's mercy, the gospel to the poor) — Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the provision of birds for the poor teach?

sides.position: The gracious provision for the poor - the way of atonement adapted to the offerer's ability, none excluded by poverty; God's mercy to the poor (the gospel preached to the poor, Matthew 11:5; Luke 4:18), the very offering the Lord's own poor parents brought at his purification (Luke 2:24), the accessibility of atonement to all

themes: And if he be not able to bring a lamb, then he shall bring... two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, unto the LORD; one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering
the provision for the poor in the trespass offering (the crux of God's mercy to the poor)
the way of atonement adapted to the offerer's ability, none excluded by poverty
the gospel to the poor, the very offering the Lord's poor parents brought (Matthew 11:5; Luke 2:24)

schools.summary: The offering of the bird for the sin offering: 'and he shall bring them unto the priest, who shall offer that which is for the sin offering first, and wring off his head from his neck, but shall not divide it asunder' — the priest offering the bird for the sin offering first, wringing off its head (pinching the neck) but not dividing the body (the sin offering first, the atonement for sin before the burnt offering of consecration, the wringing off, the killing of the bird, the blood for atonement, the not dividing, the body kept whole, the figure, the sin offering first, the atonement before the devotion, the order of the gospel); the sin offering offered first (the atonement before the burnt offering, vv.7) — the order (the sin dealt with first, then the consecration, the figure, atonement before devotion); 'wring off his head from his neck, but shall not divide it asunder' (the bird killed by wringing the neck, the blood for atonement, the body not divided, the providence, the poor's sin offering, the atonement for the poor, the figure of the death and atonement) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And he shall bring them unto the priest, who shall offer that which is for the sin offering first, and wring off his head from his neck, but shall not divide it asunder
the offering of the bird for the sin offering
the sin offering offered first, the atonement before the burnt offering of consecration (vv.7)
the figure of atonement before devotion

schools.summary: The sprinkling of the bird's blood: 'and he shall sprinkle of the blood of the sin offering upon the side of the altar; and the rest of the blood shall be wrung out at the bottom of the altar: it is a sin offering' — the priest sprinkling some of the blood on the side of the altar, and the rest being wrung out at the base (the sprinkling, the application of the blood, the atonement, the side of the altar, the blood applied, the wringing out, the rest of the blood at the base, the full giving of the life, the figure, the blood the means of atonement, the life poured out, Christ, Isaiah 53:12); the blood sprinkled on the altar (the atonement applied) — the figure (the blood of Christ, the atonement); 'the rest of the blood shall be wrung out at the bottom of the altar: it is a sin offering' (the rest of the blood at the base, the full giving of the life, the figure, the life poured out, Isaiah 53:12, the providence, the blood applied and poured, the atonement for the poor) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And he shall sprinkle of the blood of the sin offering upon the side of the altar; and the rest of the blood shall be wrung out at the bottom of the altar: it is a sin offering
the sprinkling of the bird's blood
the atonement applied and the life poured out
the figure of Christ pouring out his soul even in the poor's offering (Isaiah 53:12)

schools.summary: The burnt offering and the forgiveness for the poor: 'and he shall offer the second for a burnt offering, according to the manner: and the priest shall make an atonement for him for his sin which he hath sinned, and it shall be forgiven him' — the second bird offered for a burnt offering (the consecration), and the priest making atonement, and the sin being forgiven (the burnt offering, the second bird, the whole devotion, after the sin offering, the atonement, the covering of sin by the blood, the forgiveness, the sin forgiven, the poor pardoned as the rich, the figure, the atonement and forgiveness, the poor's full pardon through the offering); the second bird for a burnt offering (the devotion after the atonement) — the figure (the whole given to God after the sin is dealt with); 'the priest shall make an atonement for him for his sin which he hath sinned, and it shall be forgiven him' (the atonement and forgiveness, the poor pardoned, the same promise as the rich, the figure, the forgiveness through the atonement, Ephesians 1:7, the providence, the poor's atonement and forgiveness, equal with the rich) — Clarke, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And he shall offer the second for a burnt offering, according to the manner: and the priest shall make an atonement for him for his sin which he hath sinned, and it shall be forgiven him
the burnt offering and the forgiveness for the poor
the poor pardoned as the rich, the same promise of forgiveness
the figure of forgiveness through the atonement of Christ (Ephesians 1:7)

schools.summary: The fine flour offering for the very poor: 'but if he be not able to bring two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, then he that sinned shall bring for his offering the tenth part of an ephah of fine flour for a sin offering; he shall put no oil upon it, neither shall he put any frankincense thereon: for it is a sin offering' — if too poor even for the birds, the offerer might bring a tenth of an ephah of fine flour for a sin offering (the fine flour, the poorest provision, the cheapest offering, for the very poor, the further mercy, the way of atonement adapted even to the poorest, none excluded, the bloodless offering, the only sin offering without blood, exceptionally allowed for the poorest); the fine flour for the very poor (the cheapest offering, the atonement for the poorest, the further provision) — the lesson (God's mercy reaching even the poorest, none shut out, the atonement accessible to all); 'he shall put no oil upon it, neither shall he put any frankincense thereon: for it is a sin offering' (the absence of oil and frankincense, unlike the ordinary meal offering, Leviticus 2:1 — the witnesses noting the reason: it being a sin offering, not a sweet savour of joy and devotion, but for sin, the oil of gladness and the frankincense of acceptance withheld, the solemnity of sin, no rejoicing in the offering for sin, the figure, the sin offering grave, not festal, Christ made sin, the joylessness of bearing sin) — Clarke, Wesley, Gill, Ellicott.

debates.question: Why no oil or frankincense on the flour sin offering?

sides.position: Because it is a sin offering, not a sweet savour of joy and devotion - the oil of gladness and the frankincense of acceptance withheld, the solemnity of sin, no rejoicing in the offering for sin (unlike the ordinary meal offering, Leviticus 2:1); the figure of Christ made sin, the joylessness of bearing sin

themes: But if he be not able to bring two turtledoves... then he that sinned shall bring for his offering the tenth part of an ephah of fine flour for a sin offering; he shall put no oil upon it, neither shall he put any frankincense thereon: for it is a sin offering
the fine flour offering for the very poor
the atonement adapted even to the poorest, God's mercy reaching all, none excluded
no oil or frankincense because it is a sin offering, the solemnity of sin (Leviticus 2:1)

schools.summary: The burning of the memorial of the flour offering: 'then shall he bring it to the priest, and the priest shall take his handful of it, even a memorial thereof, and burn it on the altar, according to the offerings made by fire unto the LORD: it is a sin offering' — the priest taking a handful of the fine flour (the memorial portion) and burning it on the altar (the handful, the memorial, the representative portion burned to God, as in the meal offering, Leviticus 2:2, the memorial, the portion that brought the offering to remembrance before God, the burning, the offering made by fire, the rest, the remainder to the priest, vv.13, the figure, the memorial portion, the offering presented to God, even the poor's flour offering for sin); the handful, the memorial (the representative portion burned, Leviticus 2:2) — the figure (the offering presented to God); 'burn it on the altar, according to the offerings made by fire unto the LORD: it is a sin offering' (the memorial burned, the offering by fire, the sin offering of the poor, the providence, the poor's sin offering accepted, the memorial burned, the atonement) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Then shall he bring it to the priest, and the priest shall take his handful of it, even a memorial thereof, and burn it on the altar, according to the offerings made by fire unto the LORD: it is a sin offering
the burning of the memorial of the flour offering
the memorial portion burned to God, as in the meal offering (Leviticus 2:2)
the poor's sin offering accepted

schools.summary: The atonement and forgiveness, and the priest's portion: 'and the priest shall make an atonement for him as touching his sin that he hath sinned in one of these, and it shall be forgiven him: and the remnant shall be the priest's, as a meat offering' — the priest making atonement, the sin being forgiven, and the remnant of the flour going to the priest (the atonement, the covering of sin, even by the poor's flour offering, the forgiveness, the sin forgiven, the poor pardoned as the rich, the same promise, the remnant to the priest, the priest's portion, as in the meal offering, Leviticus 2:3, the figure, the atonement and forgiveness for the poorest, the equal pardon); the atonement and forgiveness (the poor pardoned, the same promise as the rich) — the figure (the forgiveness through the atonement, Ephesians 1:7, the poorest forgiven); 'the remnant shall be the priest's, as a meat offering' (the priest's portion, the remainder, as the meal offering, Leviticus 2:3, the providence, the priest fed from the offering, the atonement and forgiveness for the poor) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the priest shall make an atonement for him as touching his sin that he hath sinned in one of these, and it shall be forgiven him: and the remnant shall be the priest's, as a meat offering
the atonement and forgiveness, and the priest's portion
the poorest forgiven as the rich, the same promise (Ephesians 1:7)
the priest fed from the offering (Leviticus 2:3)

schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning the trespass offering for the holy things: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses, now to give the law of the trespass offering proper (for trespass in the holy things and against a neighbour, vv.15-19; 6:1-7, the speaking, God's command, the new section on the trespass offering, the occasion, after the sin offering and the graduated provision, now the trespass offering with its restitution); the LORD speaking (the trespass offering, distinct from the sin offering, for trespasses involving wrong and requiring restitution) — the new law (the trespass offering, the offering for wrong done, with amends); the LORD spake unto Moses — the trespass offering introduced (the providence, God giving the law of the trespass offering, the wrong to be atoned and restored) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying
the LORD speaking concerning the trespass offering for the holy things
God giving the law of the trespass offering, distinct from the sin offering (vv.15-19)
the wrong to be atoned and restored

schools.summary: The trespass offering for sin in the holy things: 'if a soul commit a trespass, and sin through ignorance, in the holy things of the LORD; then he shall bring for his trespass unto the LORD a ram without blemish out of the flocks, with thy estimation by shekels of silver, after the shekel of the sanctuary, for a trespass offering' — the holy things — 'if a soul commit a trespass... in the holy things of the LORD': if a man trespassed unwittingly in the holy things (withholding tithes, firstfruits, dues, or misusing what was sacred, Leviticus 22:14-16), he was to bring a ram for a trespass offering, with restitution (the witnesses noting the significance — the trespass in the holy things, defrauding God of his dues, the sacrilege of withholding or misusing what belongs to him, even unwittingly, the lesson — the guilt of robbing God, the holy things, the tithes and dues, belonging to God, the sin of withholding them, 'will a man rob God? yet ye have robbed me... in tithes and offerings,' Malachi 3:8, the ram and the estimation, the costly ram, valued in silver by the sanctuary shekel, the trespass requiring both sacrifice and restitution, vv.16, the figure — the trespass against God's holy things requiring both atonement and amends, the satisfaction made); the ram without blemish, valued in silver (the costly victim and the valuation, the trespass requiring satisfaction) — the figure (the unblemished ram, Christ, the satisfaction made for the wrong); the trespass in the holy things — the robbing of God (the providence, the trespass offering and restitution for the holy things, the wrong against God atoned and restored) — Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What does the trespass in the holy things teach?

sides.position: Defrauding God of his dues, the sacrilege of withholding or misusing what is sacred (Leviticus 22:14-16; Malachi 3:8) - requiring both the costly ram (a figure of Christ) and restitution (vv.16), the wrong against God's holy things atoned and made good

themes: If a soul commit a trespass, and sin through ignorance, in the holy things of the LORD; then he shall bring for his trespass unto the LORD a ram without blemish out of the flocks, with thy estimation by shekels of silver
the trespass offering for sin in the holy things (the crux of trespass and restitution)
defrauding God of his dues, the sacrilege of withholding what is sacred (Leviticus 22:14-16; Malachi 3:8)
requiring both the costly sacrifice and restitution, the wrong atoned and made good

schools.summary: The restitution with the fifth part added: 'and he shall make amends for the harm that he hath done in the holy thing, and shall add the fifth part thereto, and give it unto the priest: and the priest shall make an atonement for him with the ram of the trespass offering, and it shall be forgiven him' — the trespasser making amends (restoring what was withheld or misused), adding a fifth part (twenty per cent), and the priest making atonement, and the sin being forgiven (the restitution, the wrong made good, the holy thing restored, the fifth part added, the penalty, the restitution with increase, full satisfaction, the atonement, the ram offered, the forgiveness, the sin forgiven after both restitution and atonement); the amends — the wrong made good with the added fifth (the witnesses noting the lesson — restitution as part of repentance, the wrong not merely confessed but made good, restored with increase, Numbers 5:7; Luke 19:8, Zacchaeus, 'if I have taken any thing from any man... I restore him fourfold,' the joining of restitution and atonement, no forgiveness of the wrong without making it good, the offering not excusing the restitution, both the satisfaction to God by the ram and the restitution of the wrong, the lesson for true repentance, the fruit of repentance, the restoring of what was wrongfully taken or withheld); the atonement by the ram and the forgiveness (after restitution) — the figure (the atonement of Christ joined with the fruit of repentance, the providence, restitution and atonement, the sin forgiven) — Clarke, Wesley, JFB, Gill.

debates.question: What does the restitution with the added fifth teach?

sides.position: Restitution as the fruit of true repentance - the wrong made good with increase (Numbers 5:7; Luke 19:8), joined with the atonement by the ram (both the satisfaction to God and the restoring of the wrong, no forgiveness without making it good), the figure of repentance bearing fruit in restitution with faith in the atonement

themes: And he shall make amends for the harm that he hath done in the holy thing, and shall add the fifth part thereto... and it shall be forgiven him
the restitution with the fifth part added
the wrong made good with increase, restitution as the fruit of repentance (Numbers 5:7; Luke 19:8)
joined with the atonement by the ram, no forgiveness without making it good

schools.summary: The guilt of sin though unknown: 'and if a soul sin, and commit any of these things which are forbidden to be done by the commandments of the LORD; though he wist it not, yet is he guilty, and shall bear his iniquity' — the principle — 'though he wist it not, yet is he guilty, and shall bear his iniquity': if a man sinned against God's commandments, even without knowing it (unaware that he had sinned, or that the thing was forbidden), he was still guilty and bore his iniquity (the witnesses noting the significance — the guilt of sin even when unknown, the sin real and the guilt real, though the sinner was unaware, the objective guilt of sin, not depending on the sinner's knowledge, the lesson — the holiness of God's law and the reality of guilt, sin is sin against God whether known or not, the conscience may be ignorant but the guilt remains, 'I know nothing by myself; yet am I not hereby justified,' 1 Corinthians 4:4, the secret faults, Psalm 19:12, the need of atonement even for unknown sin, the trespass offering for the sin one does not know, the provision for guilt deeper than the conscience perceives, the figure — the need of Christ's atonement for all sin, even the unknown, the cleansing from secret faults, 1 John 1:7); the guilt borne (the iniquity real, the burden until atoned) — the providence (the trespass offering for the unknown sin, vv.18-19, the figure, the need of atonement for guilt unknown); guilty though unaware — the objective reality of sin (the providence, the atonement for unknown sin, the cleansing in Christ) — Calvin, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: How can one be guilty of sin one does not know?

sides.position: The objective guilt of sin, not depending on the sinner's knowledge - the sin and guilt real even when unaware, the holiness of God's law (the secret faults, Psalm 19:12; 1 Corinthians 4:4); the need of atonement even for unknown sin, fulfilled in Christ who cleanses from all sin, even the secret (1 John 1:7)

themes: And if a soul sin, and commit any of these things which are forbidden to be done by the commandments of the LORD; though he wist it not, yet is he guilty, and shall bear his iniquity
the guilt of sin though unknown
the objective guilt of sin, not depending on the sinner's knowledge (Psalm 19:12; 1 Corinthians 4:4)
the need of atonement even for unknown sin, the cleansing in Christ (1 John 1:7)

schools.summary: The ram for the trespass of ignorance and the forgiveness: 'and he shall bring a ram without blemish out of the flock, with thy estimation, for a trespass offering, unto the priest: and the priest shall make an atonement for him concerning his ignorance wherein he erred and wist it not, and it shall be forgiven him' — the man bringing a ram (with the valuation) for a trespass offering for the sin he did unawares, and the priest making atonement, and it being forgiven (the ram, the costly victim, as for the holy things, vv.15, the ignorance, the sin done unawares, the error not known, the atonement, the covering of the unknown sin, the forgiveness, the sin forgiven, even the unknown); the ram with the estimation (the costly victim, as for the holy things, vv.15) — the figure (the unblemished ram, Christ, the satisfaction); 'the priest shall make an atonement for him concerning his ignorance wherein he erred and wist it not, and it shall be forgiven him' (the atonement for the unknown sin and the forgiveness, the figure, the forgiveness of even unknown sin through the atonement, 1 John 1:7, the providence, the atonement and forgiveness for the sin of ignorance) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And he shall bring a ram without blemish out of the flock, with thy estimation, for a trespass offering... and it shall be forgiven him
the ram for the trespass of ignorance and the forgiveness
the costly ram for the sin done unawares, as for the holy things (vv.15)
the atonement and forgiveness for even the unknown sin (1 John 1:7)

schools.summary: The trespass against the LORD: 'it is a trespass offering: he hath certainly trespassed against the LORD' — the offering being a trespass offering, for the man had certainly (surely, truly) trespassed against the LORD (the trespass offering, the named offering, for the wrong done, the certainly, the guilt sure, the trespass real, even if unknown, the against the LORD, the trespass ultimately against God, even when the wrong was unwitting or against the holy things, the figure, all sin a trespass against God, needing atonement); the trespass offering named (the offering for the wrong) — the offering (the satisfaction for the trespass); 'he hath certainly trespassed against the LORD' (the trespass against God, the wrong ultimately against the LORD, the guilt sure, the figure, all sin against God, Psalm 51:4, 'against thee, thee only, have I sinned,' the providence, the trespass against the LORD atoned, the guilt sure and covered) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: It is a trespass offering: he hath certainly trespassed against the LORD
the trespass against the LORD
the trespass ultimately against God, the guilt sure
all sin against the LORD needing atonement (Psalm 51:4)
